1: The LORD commands Moses in the plains of Moab: command the Israelites to give to the Levites towns to live in from the inheritance they possess, as well as pasturelands around the towns. The Levites receive dispersed cities rather than contiguous territory — they are present throughout all the tribes rather than concentrated in one region. The church as a priestly people is similarly dispersed throughout all nations rather than confined to a single geography (Matt 28:19; 1 Pet 2:9).
2: Give the Levites towns to live in and pastureland around the towns for their livestock. The provision of both habitation and pasture — settled dwelling plus room for their flocks — models God's comprehensive provision for those who serve him full-time. Christ promises that those who seek first the kingdom will have all necessary things added (Matt 6:33); the church has a responsibility to provide materially for those engaged in full-time ministry (1 Cor 9:14).
3: The towns shall be for them to live in, and their pasturelands shall be for their livestock, for their goods, and for all their animals. The Levitical cities are comprehensively provisioned — home, pasture, and storage. God's care for his servants extends to every practical need of life. Christ instructs his disciples: 'The laborer deserves his wages' (Luke 10:7), and the early church implemented this by sharing material resources (Acts 2:44–45).
4: The pasturelands of the towns shall extend 1,000 cubits around from the wall of the city. The prescribed measurement creates a buffer zone of commons around each Levitical city — a shared resource zone. The principle of common good around the priestly community anticipates the church as a community that creates a zone of hospitality, welcome, and shared life around itself (Heb 13:2).
5: You shall measure outside the city 2,000 cubits to the east, 2,000 to the south, 2,000 to the west, and 2,000 to the north — with the city in the middle. The Levitical city at the center of its surrounding pasture is a spatial image of the priestly community as the center of the surrounding landscape. The church is called to be the center of spiritual gravity in its community — the place of worship, justice, and care around which civic life is oriented.
6: The towns you give to the Levites shall include the six cities of refuge — where you shall permit the manslayer to flee — plus forty-two other towns. The cities of refuge are embedded within the larger category of Levitical cities. Christ as the refuge is also the High Priest — the two functions are inseparable: safe access to God requires both the priestly mediation (Levitical city) and the protective refuge (city of asylum) that Christ provides (Heb 4:14–16).
7: All the towns you give the Levites shall be 48 towns, together with their pasturelands. Forty-eight Levitical cities distributed throughout all of Israel — approximately four per tribe — ensures a priestly presence across the entire land. The church distributed throughout every people group and nation similarly ensures that the priestly ministry of prayer, word, and sacrament reaches every corner of human civilization.
8: The towns given from the property of the Israelites shall be in proportion: from a large tribe you give many, and from a small tribe few. Each gives according to ability. The proportional distribution of Levitical cities models the principle of proportional giving: 'The one who sows bountifully will also reap bountifully' (2 Cor 9:6); those with more resources support the priestly community more.
9: The LORD says to Moses: speak to the Israelites: when you cross the Jordan into Canaan, designate some towns as cities of refuge. The city of refuge exists in anticipation of the crossing — it is planned for before the land is occupied. Christ's redemption was planned before the foundation of the world (Eph 1:4; Rev 13:8), the refuge prepared before any sinner needed to flee there.
10: Tell the Israelites: when you cross the Jordan into the land of Canaan, appoint cities to be cities of refuge for you. The purpose is clear and immediate: for you — these cities are for your benefit and safety. Christ is for his people: his refuge is personal, not merely institutional. 'God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble' (Ps 46:1); Christ applies this personally to every sinner who flees to him.
11: Designate the cities as places of refuge to which a person who has killed someone accidentally may flee. The manslayer (one who kills accidentally, without premeditation) is the specific beneficiary of the city of refuge. Christ's refuge is available to those whose sin, though real, was not the full-measure malice of the murderer — and even for those whose sin was intentional, his cross provides the ultimate refuge from wrath (Rom 5:8).
12: The cities shall be places of refuge from the avenger, so that the one who kills may not die before standing trial before the assembly. The city provides a pause — not exoneration but due process, protection while the case is heard. Christ as refuge does not ignore sin but provides the space for proper adjudication. His atoning work is not a suppression of justice but its fulfillment: 'that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus' (Rom 3:26).
13: These six towns shall be your cities of refuge. Six cities — three east and three west of the Jordan — ensure that no place in the entire land is too far from refuge. The accessibility of the city of refuge models the accessibility of Christ: 'everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved' (Rom 10:13). The distance to the nearest city of refuge was never supposed to be prohibitive.
14: Appoint three cities east of the Jordan and three in the land of Canaan; they shall be cities of refuge. The symmetrical distribution — three east, three west — ensures equal access regardless of which side of the Jordan one inhabits. Christ is equally accessible to Jew and Gentile, insider and outsider: 'there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; for the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him' (Rom 10:12).
15: These six cities shall be for refuge for the people of Israel and for the stranger and for the sojourner among them, so that anyone who kills any person without intent may flee there. The city of refuge is open to all — Israelite, resident alien, and traveler. The non-Israelite can access the same protective covenant institution as the native. Christ's refuge is universal: 'For God so loved the world' (John 3:16), no ethnic or national qualifier on the welcome.
16: But if someone strikes another person with an iron implement and death results, that is murder — the murderer shall be put to death. The distinction between the manslayer and the murderer rests on intent and instrumentality. Iron weapons suggest premeditation. Christ distinguishes between different gradations of guilt: the servant who knew the master's will and did not do it will receive a worse beating than one who did not know (Luke 12:47–48). Judgment is proportional.
17: If someone strikes another person with a stone implement that could cause death — that is murder, and the murderer shall be put to death. The stone implement test reflects practical wisdom: a weapon capable of killing, used with intent, constitutes murder. Christ's law goes deeper, identifying the murderous heart behind even the unexecuted act: 'everyone who is angry with his brother will be liable to judgment' (Matt 5:22).
18: Or if someone strikes another with a wooden weapon that could cause death — that is murder. The comprehensive coverage of weapon types ensures no legal loophole. Christ closes every moral loophole in his own teaching: lust is adultery (Matt 5:28), hatred is murder (1 John 3:15). The law's letter is completed by the law's spirit in Christ's teaching.
19: The blood-avenger himself may put the murderer to death; when he meets him, he may kill him. The blood-avenger (gōʾēl haddām) is the kinsman-redeemer acting as executor of justice for his slain relative. Christ is the ultimate gōʾēl: the kinsman-redeemer who vindicates his people against every adversary, including death itself (Job 19:25; Ruth 4:6–10 as typological background).
20: If someone with hatred kills another person by pushing them or throwing something at them from ambush. Premeditation plus hatred: the two elements that constitute murder in its most culpable form. Christ traces this pattern back to its origin in the human heart: 'out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder...' (Matt 15:19). The city of refuge was not designed for those who plan their sins in advance.
21: Or if in enmity someone strikes another person down with their hand and death results, that is murder. The blood-avenger may execute the murderer upon encounter. Enmity as the root of murder is John's application: 'Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer' (1 John 3:15). Christ, who was struck down in enmity by those who hated him, bears the ultimate judgment of enmity so that those who killed him in hatred might be forgiven (Acts 2:23–24; Luke 23:34).
22: But if someone pushes another person suddenly without enmity, or throws something at them accidentally. The involuntary, unpremeditated nature of the killing — without enmity and without prior plotting — defines the manslayer eligible for refuge. The distinction between accident and intention is foundational to human legal reasoning; Christ's atoning work covers both categories, though with different conditions for each.
23: Or without seeing them, lets fall a stone that could kill — and the person dies — though they were not enemies and no harm was intended. The case of purely accidental killing — no weapon, no intent, no enmity — represents the manslayer at the furthest remove from murderous guilt. The city of refuge exists even for this extreme case of accidental death: grace reaches to the edges of even the least culpable human fault.
24: The assembly shall judge between the one who killed and the blood-avenger according to these rules. The assembly as judge — the community applying the law — models the communal adjudication that characterizes covenant justice. Christ promises that his church will share in the final judgment: 'Do you not know that the saints will judge the world?' (1 Cor 6:2).
25: The assembly shall protect the manslayer from the blood-avenger and return him to the city of refuge to which he fled, and he shall live there until the death of the high priest who was anointed with the holy oil. The critical provision: confinement in the city lasts until the high priest's death — then the manslayer goes free. This is the most explicit typological pointer to Christ's death as the mechanism of release from the law's constraints. The death of the High Priest — Jesus Christ, anointed with the Spirit — frees every manslayer who has fled to him for refuge (Heb 9:15).
26: But if the manslayer ever goes outside the boundary of his city of refuge to which he has fled. The condition for losing protection: leaving the refuge voluntarily. Those who abandon the place of safety place themselves back under the jurisdiction of the blood-avenger. The warning of Hebrews applies: those who deliberately go on sinning after receiving the knowledge of the truth 'have trampled the Son of God underfoot' — they have left the city of refuge (Heb 10:26–29).
27: And the blood-avenger finds him outside the city of refuge and kills him — the avenger is not guilty of blood. The blood-avenger has no legal liability for killing the manslayer who has left the refuge. The terrifying logic of this provision makes Christ's refuge the only safety: outside of Christ, the wrath of the divine avenger has just grounds against every sinner. The warning is meant to make believers cling to the city of refuge rather than wander from it.
28: For the manslayer must remain in his city of refuge until the death of the high priest; after the death of the high priest, the manslayer may return to the land of his possession. The release from the city after the high priest's death represents a new freedom — the freedom of those whom Christ's death has fully liberated. 'So if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed' (John 8:36): the death of the High Priest Jesus Christ is the release from all legal claims against the believer.
29: These things shall be a statute and rule for you throughout your generations in all your dwelling places. The city of refuge law is perpetual — it applies wherever Israel lives. Its new-covenant fulfillment in Christ is equally universal and perpetual: the refuge of Christ is available to all people in all places for all time. 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever' (Heb 13:8).
30: If anyone kills a person, the murderer shall be put to death on the evidence of witnesses. But no person shall be put to death on the testimony of one witness. The two-witness rule for capital punishment — protecting the innocent from false accusation — is the foundation of the biblical legal system and Jesus's own practice of witness-discernment. Christ himself fulfills the two-witness requirement in his resurrection: 'He presented himself alive after his suffering by many proofs' (Acts 1:3).
31: Moreover, you shall accept no ransom for the life of a murderer who is guilty of death, but he shall be put to death. No money redemption for murder — life cannot be purchased back once forfeited by willful killing. Yet Christ provides what no ransom payment can: a life for a life, his own blood for the blood of all who deserve the murderer's penalty. His ransom is not money but himself: 'a ransom for many' (Mark 10:45).
32: And you shall accept no ransom for the manslayer who has fled to his city of refuge, that he might return to live in the land before the death of the priest. The ban on ransom for the manslayer who wants early release prevents wealthy people from buying their way out of the protective confinement. The refuge is equally binding on rich and poor. Christ's atonement is equally effective for rich and poor: 'there is no partiality with God' (Rom 2:11).
33: You shall not pollute the land in which you live, for blood pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it. Blood defiles the land — only the death of the murderer cleanses it. This principle of blood-atonement-for-blood-shedding is the structural foundation of the entire sacrificial system, fulfilled in Christ: 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness' (Heb 9:22). Christ's blood is the only adequate atonement for the blood-polluted earth.
34: You shall not defile the land in which you live, in the midst of which I dwell, for I the LORD dwell in the midst of the people of Israel. The ultimate reason for maintaining the land's purity: YHWH himself dwells there. The land is holy because the holy God inhabits it. In the new covenant, the Spirit dwells within God's people, making them the holy temple that must be kept pure: 'Do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you? If anyone destroys God's temple, God will destroy him' (1 Cor 3:16–17).
